lessonTitle: Improving Health Equity for Pregnant People with SCD

items.html: Identify barriers to care for pregnant people with Sickle Cell Disease
List proactive means of improving health equity for pregnant people with Sickle Cell Disease

slides.title: Health Equity for Pregnant People with Sickle Cell Disease

items.html: We have learned throughout the modules that people with SCD experience barriers to care that include racism, discrimination, and stigma.

In this lesson we will highlight some of the ongoing barriers faced by pregnant people with SCD, but also identify ways clinicians can mitigate obstetric racism and promote health equity strategies for improved patient care and outcomes.

Definition: When Black women are trying to access maternal health services and they are subjected to beliefs and practices that lend to the reproducing Black body being harmed and manifests as forms of violence and abuse.
History: Historically, Black women became victims of gynaecological surgeries without anaesthesia that were experimental, due to the racist belief that Black people have a high pain tolerance.
Perpetuation: This has set the precedence for how Black childbearing women are expected to be treated within the healthcare system and by healthcare providers.

slides.title: Why Are We Talking about Racism in Obstetrical Care?

data.html:
According to Early et al. (2023) there were 3 categories for pregnant people with SCD that demonstrated statically significant differences from the control group:

  • Hypertensive disorders of pregnancy
  • Labor and delivery complications
  • Hematologic and immunologic complications

The maternal mortality rate in deliveries among people with SCD was 26 times greater than in the non-Black control group and more than 10 times greater than among Black pregnant people without SCD

slides.title: Quote on Racism as Determinant of Health

data.bubble_text: Racism as a determinant of health, along with the intersection of class, gender, and access to health services, adversely affects Black women's health outcomes

rows: Standing in proximity to healthcare providers, their presence were unacknowledged
Prejudice about Black women's capacities to endure pain led to healthcare providers refusing to provide pain relief during labour
Women reported lack of compassion and loss of humanness in care
Decisions made for them and not with them. Lack of control and ability to influence their own care
Participants felt an impending sense of failure and feelings of guilt from the internalization of stigma experienced from healthcare providers

slides.title: Addressing Racial Disparities For Pregnant Patients

items.html: Engage with your institution's anti-racism frameworks and policies
Consider professional development courses in anti-oppressive frameworks
Engage multidisciplinary allies in maternity care, such as social workers with trauma-informed care expertise
Speak openly with patients about Black maternal health outcomes. Encourage participation in shared care and benefits of participating in maternal research, where available

slides.title: Reduction of Peripartum Racial and Ethnic Disparities

data.html:
• In an effort to reduce peripartum racial and ethnic disparities, a workgroup made up of representatives from Obstetrics, Maternal Fetal Medicine, Midwives, Maternal/Child, Family Physicians, and a School of Public Health was developed
  • This workgroup focused on the development of an evidence-based patient safety bundle.
  • The proposed bundle was to be feasible and actionable, and have the potential to improve the quality of maternal health care, while reducing racial and ethnic disparities in maternal morbidity and mortality.
  • Their work culminated in the development of a bundle that can be used by those who find them of benefit.
  • Resource: Reduction of Peripartum Racial and Ethnic Disparities Bundle: https://saferbirth.org/wp-content/uploads/Reduction-of-Peripartum-Racial-and-Ethnic-Disparities_FINAL-11.7.16.pdf

Addressing Racial Disparities

To provide the highest quality care to patients, we must deliver care that is free of bias and includes:

  • Speaking openly with patients about Black maternal health outcomes
  • Professional development courses in anti-oppressive framework
  • Engaging with your institution's anti-racism frameworks and policies

questions.question: My knowledge about Fertility, Contraception, and Pregnancy in Sickle Cell Disease on a scale of 1 to 10 is ___ .
My comfort level with Fertility, Contraception, and Pregnancy in SCD on a scale of 1 to 10 is ___ .
My confidence level with Fertility, Contraception, and Pregnancy for people with SCD on a scale of 1 to 10 is ___ .

items.html: Racism, discrimination, and stigma all negatively impact the experiences of pregnant people with Sickle Cell Disease

Strategies such as aligning with your institutions anti-racism policy, attending continuing education, and engaging multi disciplinary team members in the care of pregnant people with SCD, will improve outcomes and experiences

Boakye, P., Prendergast, N., Bandari, B., Brown, E., Odutayo, A., and Salami, S. (2023). Obstetric racism and perceived quality of maternity care in Canada: Voices of Black women. Women's Health, 19. 1-13.

Early, M. BA; Ahizechukwu C. Eke, MBChB, PhD; Alison Gemmill, PhD; Sophie Lanzkron, MD, MHS; Lydia H. Pecker, MD, MHS. (2023). Severe Maternal Morbidity and Mortality in Sickle Cell Disease in the National Inpatient Sample, 2012-2018. Jama Network Open, 6(2)
